mnemonics.title: CAP Theorem for Cassandra
mnemonics.content: AP in CAP - Always Partition-tolerant, choose Availability over Consistency. Remember: 'Available even with Problems' = AP = Cassandra's design philosophy.

mnemonics.title: Write Path Memory
mnemonics.content: COMMIT to LOG, then MEMTable - Writes go to Commit Log (durability) first, then MemTable (memory). Memory to Disk = SSTable.

mnemonics.title: Data Distribution
mnemonics.content: PARTITION KEY determines WHERE - The partition key decides node placement using Murmur3 or other partitioner. Row key = Row's address!

mnemonics.title: Consistency Levels
mnemonics.content: ONE = 1, QUORUM = majority, ALL = everyone. Think: 'One' is fastest but least consistent, 'All' is slowest but most consistent.

acronyms.usage: SQL-like language for interacting with Cassandra databases, CREATE KEYSPACE, SELECT, INSERT operations
Immutable on-disk file format in Cassandra that stores data persistently after MemTable flush
Expiration time for data, automatically deleted after specified seconds; useful for temporary data
Commit Log in Cassandra that ensures durability by persisting writes before memory operations

acronyms.expansion: Consistent And Scalable, Service Available with Non-synchronous Replication and Architecture
acronyms.usage: Backronym for remembering Cassandra's AP nature with eventual consistency
Category of databases that provide flexible schemas and horizontal scalability
Database design where rows can have many columns with different names per row

visualTips.visualization: Draw two boxes: Row storage = horizontal arrangement (all fields of one record together), Column storage = vertical arrangement (all values of one field together). Think of a spreadsheet: row storage = row by row, column storage = column by column reading.
Imagine a clock face (ring) where each number represents a node with a token range. Data flows clockwise from one node to the next based on partition key hash. No central master - all nodes equal!
Think of water: Memory (MemTable) flows down to disk (SSTable) and hardens into stone (immutable). Multiple small stones (SSTables) merge into bigger ones (compaction).
Scale with two hands: MORE consistency = hand grip on data (slower, more coordination), MORE availability = open hands (faster, more independent). Can't hold both tight!

analogies.analogy: Think of a library: Row database = books with complete information (one book per reader), Column database = index cards organized by author, another by subject, another by year - efficient for finding 'all books by author X'.

Like social media posts: When you post, it's on your phone (one server). It syncs to friends' phones (other servers) over seconds/minutes. Eventually everyone sees it, but immediately after posting, some might not see it yet.
Like postal zip codes: The zip code (partition key) determines which regional post office (node) handles your letter (row data). Same zip = same destination.

Like a dictionary book: Once printed (immutable), you don't change it. New words go in a separate supplement (new MemTable/SSTable). Periodically, you merge supplements into a new edition (compaction).
